original_abstract:
Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was successfully produced as a sustainable solar fuel from water and dioxygen by combining a water oxidation catalyst with a photocatalyst capable of two-electron O2 reduction in acidic media. Under visible light irradiation, the photocatalyst [RuII(Me2phen)3]2+ (Me2phen = 4,7-dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline) facilitated H2O2 production in O2-saturated aqueous solutions containing Ir(OH)3 as a water oxidation catalyst. The process is initiated by photoinduced electron transfer from the excited state of the ruthenium complex to O2, yielding [RuIII(Me2phen)3]3+ and a superoxide radical anion (O2˙−). Subsequent protonation and disproportionation of the resulting HO2˙ radicals generate H2O2, a pathway that competes with back electron transfer (BET) from O2˙− to the Ru(III) species. Following the reduction step, [RuIII(Me2phen)3]3+ oxidizes water back to O2 with the assistance of the Ir(OH)3 catalyst. 

The efficiency of this system was significantly enhanced by replacing Ir(OH)3 nanoparticles with the homogeneous catalyst [CoIII(Cp*)(bpy)(H2O)]2+ in the presence of Sc(NO3)3. Detailed kinetic studies revealed that Sc3+ acts as a Lewis acid to inhibit BET, thereby stabilizing the intermediate species. Using a ferrioxalate actinometer at λ = 450 nm, the optimized photocatalytic system achieved a quantum yield of 37% and a solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiency of 0.25%, demonstrating a promising approach for the direct solar-driven synthesis of liquid fuels from earth-abundant resources.
humanized_abstract:
Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was produced as a solar fuel from water and dioxygen utilizing a combination of a water oxidation catalyst and a photocatalyst for two-electron reduction of O2. By mixing the photocatalyst [RuII(Me2phen)3]2+ (Me2phen = 4,7-dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline) and a water oxidation catalyst Ir(OH)3 in an acidic solution, it was found that irradiation of the solution with visible light gave H2O2 under O2 atmosphere. Initially, a photoinduced electron transfer is believed to occur from the excited-state of the reduced ruthenium(II) complex to dioxygen forming the oxidized ruthenium(III) species [RuIII(Me2phen)3]3+ and the superoxide anion radical, O2˙−. Subsequent protonation and disproportionation of the HO2˙ gives H2O2 and the cycle continues by subsequent reduction of O2 via the catalytic cycle including the Ir(OH)3 catalyst.

Using nanoparticles of Ir(OH)3 as a catalyst, results were mediocre at best. Replacing the nanoparticles with the homogeneous catalyst [CoIII(Cp*)(bpy)(H2O)]2+ in the presence of a small amount of Sc(NO3)3, significantly improved the performance of the system and revealed the role of Sc3+ as a Lewis acid that suppresses the back electron transfer (BET) and thus, stabilized the intermediate species formed during the process. Under illumination with sunlight, using a ferrioxalate actinometer at λ = 450 nm, the photocatalytic system achieved a quantum yield of 37% and solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiency of 0.25% for the production of liquid fuel at near atmospheric pressure from earth abundant feedstocks.
